                                                                 Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


                                                                    Contents lists available at ScienceDirect


                                                            Egyptian Informatics Journal
                                                               journal homepage: www.sciencedirect.com




AI-Driven livestock identification and insurance management system
Munir Ahmad a, Sagheer Abbas a, Areej Fatima b, Taher M. Ghazal c, d, Meshal Alharbi e,
Muhammad Adnan Khan f, *, Nouh Sabri Elmitwally g, h
a
  School of Computer Science, National College of Business Administration & Economics, Lahore 54000, Pakistan
b
  Department of Computer Science, Lahore Garrison University, Lahore 54000, Pakistan
c
  School of Information Technology, Skyline University College, University City Sharjah, Sharjah 1797, UAE
d
  Applied Science Research Center, Applied Science Private University, Amman 11931, Jordan
e
  Department of Computer Science, College of Computer Engineering and Sciences, Prince Sattam Bin Abdulaziz University, Alkharj 11942, Saudia Arabia
f
  Department of Software, Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Software, Gahcon University, Seongnam 13120, Republic of Korea
g
  School of Computing and Digital Technology, Birmingham City University, Birmingham B4 7XG, UK
h
  Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Computers and Artificial Intelligence, Cairo University, Giza 12613, Egypt




A R T I C L E I N F O                                     A B S T R A C T

Keywords:                                                 Cattle identification is pivotal for many reasons. Animal health management, traceability, bread classification,
Machine Learning                                          and verification of insurance claims are largely depended on the accurate identification of the animals.
Transfer Learning                                         Conventionally, animals have been identified by various means such as ear tags, tattoos, rumen implants, and hot
Deep Learning
                                                          brands. Being non-scientific approaches, these controls can be easily circumvented. The emerging technologies of
Artificial Intelligence
                                                          biometric identification are extensively applied for Human recognition via thumb impression, face features, or
                                                          eye retina patterns. The application of biometric recognition technology has now moved towards animals. Cattle
                                                          identification with the help of muzzle patterns has shown tremendous results. For precise identification, nature
                                                          has awarded a unique Muzzle pattern that can be utilized as a primary biometric feature. Muzzle pattern image
                                                          scanning for biometric identification has now been extensively applied for identification. Animal recognition via
                                                          Muzzle pattern image for different applications has been proliferating gradually. One of those applications in­
                                                          cludes the identification of fake insurance claims under livestock insurance. Fraudulent animal owners tend to
                                                          lodge fake claims against livestock insurance with proxy animals. In this paper, we proposed the solution to avoid
                                                          and/or discard fraudulent claims of livestock insurance by intelligently identifying the proxy animals. Data
                                                          collection of animal muzzle patterns remained challenging. Key aspects of the proposed system include: (1) the
                                                          Animal face will be detected through visual using YOLO v7 object detector. (2) After face detection, the same
                                                          procedures will apply to detect muzzle point (3) the muzzle pattern is extracted and then stored in the database.
                                                          The System has a mean average precision of 100% for the face and 99.43% for the nose/muzzle point of the
                                                          animal. Once the animal is registered in the database, the identification process is initiated by extracting unique
                                                          nose pattern features with ORB and/or SIFT. Then it is matched using the pattern matchers like BFMatcher and/
                                                          or FLANNMatcher for animal identification. The proposed model is more efficient and accurate as compared to
                                                          concurrent approaches. The results extracted from this research study show 100% accurate identification.




1. Introduction                                                                                accurate identification is of utmost importance. Cattle identification can
                                                                                               broadly be categorized into two types namely contact and non-contact
   Nature provided human beings with countless blessings and animals                           methods [2]. Under the contact method, various techniques have been
are one of those. They are beneficial in many ways as a source of                              used manually to recognize animals such as hot iron branding, freeze
nurtured food like meat, dairy products, and clothing such as woolen                           branding, ear tags, rumen implants, tattoos, etc. [3]. Though conven­
and leather [1].                                                                               tional methods served the purpose to some extent but with a major
   To protect the Cattle effectively from multiple hazards, their                              pitfall of easy circumvention [4]. Contact methods inherent major

drawback of Animal stress due to the painful procedure involved to mark           labeling/ notation of images and then splitting data into train and test
identity. However, these are invasive for the cattle and non-cost-                data.
efficient as compared to other more effective processes [5]. Non-                     The proposed system will intelligently detect the face and muzzle
contact methods include AI-based technologies using biometric identi­             point of the cattle, and identify/recognize the cattle using muzzle pat­
fication and other machine learning models.                                       terns in real-time.
    Cattle identification is equally important for Livestock Risk coverage.           The system will not only be helpful for the detection of false insur­
Insurers are highly concerned about the exact recognition of insurable            ance claims but it can also be utilized on farms for monitoring the cattle,
animals; not only to provide adequate cover but also to establish a firm          for their health, safety, and management. The traditional approaches to
basis for subsequent verification. Cattle identification using conven­            recognizing cattle except for the naked eye are tagging the animal using
tional approaches remain a significant issue for livestock insurers and           ear tags, and implanting microchips in the animal [13]. While micro­
veterinarian for registration and health monitoring activities.                   chips like NFC, RFID, etc. are better than ear tags, they can be expensive,
    Manual processing of cattle identification is considered highly               and hurtful to the cattle as they will be needed to be implanted inside the
vulnerable to fake insurance claims that can’t be detected accurately             cattle and there will be a need for an expert who can safely execute the
[4].                                                                              said procedure, which can be expensive. On the other hand, using ear
    During the last couple of decades, with the evolvement of AI tech­            tags is not very efficient as these tags can be forged or switched from one
nologies such as machine learning / deep learning, human recognition              animal to other pretty easily.
using human biometrics features of the face, iris, fingerprint, etc. It has           The proposed system offers wide range of potential applications,
become quite easy to recognize human beings with greater accuracy [6].            including the accurate and efficient tracking of individual cattle for
The recognition using biometrics is not limited to humans anymore and             breeding or medical purposes, as well as monitoring their behavior and
now animal recognition has also become a vital segment of this                    movement within feedlots, and identifying lost or stolen animals. This
emerging technology [7].                                                          novel approach has the potential to revolutionize cattle management
    The cattle identification system could be used to fulfill a variety           and tracking, resulting in increased productivity and efficiency within
claims, animal traceability, registration, monitoring, and.                       the agricultural sector. By utilizing advanced computer vision tech­
    Animal Registration through a Biometric system, based on scientific           niques, the proposed system provides a rapid and precise method for
techniques is widely used for Cattle identification and traceability [8]. A       identifying specific cattle based on their unique biometric characteris­
highly transparent process of cattle registration would mitigate the risk         tics, offering significant advantages over traditional identification
of manipulation, fraudulent verification, and cattle swapping. The Cattle         methods.

manage fake livestock Insurance claims [11]. Farm owners who have                 researches.
insured their cattle, unfortunately, tend to make false claims because a
non-technical layman who did not stay around the cattle so much,                  2. Literature review
cannot easily recognize the cattle. Only those who stay for a longer
period and are appropriately familiar with the cattle can identify which              During the last couple of decades, numerous research studies have

human fingerprints, cattle muzzle print comprises two unique features             Intelligence (AI) has made the task much easier to identify any cattle
recognized as ridge bifurcation and ridge termination which can be used           with greater accuracy, minimal effort, and shorter time [15]. Since
to track accurate Identity by matching relevant information with pre­             ancient times, various techniques have been employed for accurate
served datasets.                                                                  identifications of cattle. Conventional systems of cattle recognition
    The input parameters of the proposed system comprise three layers             include Hot or cold branding, ear tagging, ear tattoos, and ear notches
namely Data Acquisition, Pre-processing, and Data Preparation. The                [16]. Though these are comparatively cheaper the process is very
Data Acquisition Layer represents the source from which the data is               paining for cattle which often causes severe health issues for the animals
firstly pooled in the system. In this segment, two activities will have           [16].
been performed namely collection of raw images of animals and then                    Alternatively, the Biometric system uses visual features to identify an
storing cluster datasets into the database management system. We also             individual animal. Biometric features may include iris patterns, muzzle
use a video dataset to enhance accuracy. The Number of frames in the              images, and eye retina. These methods though introduce computerized
video sequence impacts the identification results.                                systems but they also involve some critical challenges, such as accuracy
    Pre-processing is an essential layer applied to extract image features        based on extracted features and time required for process completion.
and subsequent matching procedures for object recognition [12]. The               Anders Herlin et al. [17] highlighted the usage of digital technologies
prime objectives to be achieved in the pre-processing segment are to              such as GPS collars, E-tagging, and Chips used for RFID and its impli­
minimize though not eliminate the noises and other particles muzzle               cations for the management and monitoring of cattle farms.
images. Next to data collection, data will be purified by applying certain            The recent work in cattle face recognition has brought forth multiple
processes. Major activities In Preprocessing segment include to Remove            techniques to recognize cattle faces. In earlier days of research on cattle
Blurry and noisy images, Cropping and Resizing of images. Data Prep­              biometrics features, Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) technique
aration is the final stage of Data input for the biometric system of Cattle       had been used to extract muzzle patterns features from image print [18].
identification. Purified data received from preprocessing layer will              The implanted RFID chips are also used for tracking and identification of
finally tag in the Data preparation layer. Activities performed include           cattle. However, it is challenging for large herds to implement RFID

time, and from there, they recognize ear tags using image segmentation              • Data Acquisition
techniques. The problem with this technique is the ear tags that can be             • Data Preprocessing
altered and forged. Hongyu Wang et al. [25] proposed a parametric                   • Data Augmentation
transfer approach with VGGFace dataset and VGG-16 deep convolu­                     • Data Preparation
tional neural network. Pre-trained VGG-16 network is then fed with
their cattle face dataset. Their dataset contains images of cattle faces,
which again is not a very efficient way to recognize cattle, especially            3.1. Data Acquisition
buffalos, as in low-lit environments, their facial features cannot be
differentiated. Santosh Kumar et al. [26] proposed a system to recognize               Raw images of cattle were taken using different smartphones with
cattle in real-time. The proposed system recognizes cattle using the               different camera capabilities. The environments in which the images
muzzle point or nose pattern of the cattle. It involves: (1) the cattle is         were taken were different too, to add diversity to image data. These
captured through a video camera in real-time, (2) the frames are then              include indoor, outdoor, sunny, and cloudy weather conditions. The
cropped into nose/muzzle point images (3) which are then preprocessed              cattle include domestic buffalos, cows, calves, and bulls. Taking pictures
further to remove any noise or blurry images and converted into gray­              of these animals was the most difficult part of data acquisition as it
scale images, (4) then their features are extracted using appearance-              required a lot of hard work and running after the animals. This struggle
based feature extractor algorithms, (5) which are then stored into the             resulted in a lot of useless images, a few of which can be seen in Fig. 1.
database. In their testing phase, they take the nose image as a query              The difficulty in taking the pictures of cattle was that the animals would
image and then extract features using FLLP extractor. At this point, the           get scared the moment a person goes near to grab their picture, as it is
features are matched with the features in the database, where a                    required for the dataset that the images of cattle should be clear and
threshold value is returned, which is compared with a predefined                   have their head/face in it. Another problem that occurred was that,
threshold value to determine the decision of cattle recognition. The               because some of the cattle were eating/grazing, their nose/muzzle point
problem with this method is that nose images are cropped from sur­                 was covered in grass straws, hence taking such images would be of no
veillance video frames. Santosh Kumar et al. [27] presented a compre­              use. Even though it was the priority to collect clear and concise images of
hensive review of cattle identification along with problems/drawbacks              cattle, a lot of useless images were still captured along with the required
involve in various methods, they emphasized on development of a                    images. Images of animals including, cows, buffalos, bulls, and calves
framework for cattle identification via muzzle point. Table 1 has the              along with images other than these were collected, which included
comparison of proposed solution with related work by other researchers.            humans, dogs, sheep, horses, and donkeys, etc. A dataset of 300 animals
                                                                                   from Shojaeipour, Ali et al. [28] was also added in the dataset which
3. Research methodology                                                            totaled in 5875 images, which were not much, as the dataset still
                                                                                   required some cleansing.
     The proposed system to intelligently identify cattle in real-time or in
still images or videos requires an image dataset of cattle to be trained so        3.2. Data Preprocessing
that it can detect the face/head and nose of cattle. The process of col­
lecting image datasets for training and testing consists of the following              Once the data was collected, the images were then filtered out. Some
steps, which include:                                                              irrelevant visuals which are considered noise in the data can be seen in
                                                                                   Fig. 1, had to be deleted, some images needed cropping, and some




                                                                    Fig. 1. Noise in Data.

                                                                               4
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                           Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


images needed to be resized. This step was necessary to have images that            Table 2
are required to properly train the system.                                          Data Distribution and Class Labels.
                                                                                     Class           Train                  Validation             Test
3.3. Data augmentation                                                                               Image      Label       Image      Label       Image       Label

                                                                                     All             8820       14,463      480         786        100         167
    Data augmentation is a technique where, when required, more data                 Face            8820       4945        480         269        100         56
is generated from existing data if the existing data is not enough. That is          Nose            8820       3342        480         181        100         39
                                                                                     Nose-Dirty      8820       2878        480         178        100         38
the case with our dataset and thus in order to increase the data set a bit,
                                                                                     Not-Cow         8820       3298        480         158        100         34
data augmentation was used. The augmentation steps include: hori­
zontal flip, − 10 degrees to 10 degrees rotation, crop, grayscale, hue,
brightness, exposure, noise and saturation. Doing this substantially                as cow/buffalo faces, and in some case, they even detect human faces so
increased the amount of the dataset.                                                Not-Cow class is used as shown in Table 2 to make sure the model un­
                                                                                    derstands the difference, and to make sure the subject which is a Cow/
3.4. Data Preparation                                                               Buffalo is in the scene and its face is being captured to get its nose
                                                                                    pattern.
    Now when the data is preprocessed, and augmented the next step                      Class label balance can be seen in the following analysis of training
was to prepare it for training and testing. Our purpose is to detect the            data, Fig. 3. Total annotated dataset comprises on 15,416 class labels
face/head, nose of cattle, dirty nose, and faces other than cows/buffalos.          representing all the four classes.
So, for detection in images, image data has to be annotated to be trained
for object detection. A total of 9400 images were annotated using the               3.5. Training
open source image Labeling Tool [29]. Once annotated, it had to be split
into training ~ 94%, validation ~ 5%, and testing 1 %, Fig. 2.                         Now that we have our dataset annotated and prepared as shown in
    We have used five classes namely: Face, Nose, Nose-Dirty, and Not-              Fig. 4, the dataset is now ready to be trained.
cow, Table 2.

                                                                                    3.6. Detecto
 • Face:

                                                                                        We used a pre-trained Fast R-CNN ResNet-50 FPN model-based py­
   The class represents cattle face. It is necessary to detect cattle face to
                                                                                    thon package Detecto [30]. It is a python package based on PyTorch and
locate muzzle point, and to make sure a cow or buffalo is present in the
                                                                                    can be used for the training of object detection. However, it did not
scene.
                                                                                    prove to be efficient for our problem even though above 80% accuracy
                                                                                    was achieved Fig. 5 shows training loss. But when the model was tested
 • Nose:
                                                                                    it would detect the face with above 90% accuracy.
                                                                                        It seemed to mostly leave the nose part out, which would be a
  Detection of Nose/Muzzle Point is the ultimate objective to extract
                                                                                    problem as our main goal is to detect the nose once the face was
muzzle pattern for biometric identification of the cattle, thus Nose class.
                                                                                    detected, Fig. 6. Detecto model does not properly predict the bounding
                                                                                    box. The bounding box predicted by the model is way off the required
 • Nose-Dirty:
                                                                                    subject which in our case is the cattle head/face, its nose, either dirty or
                                                                                    not, and faces other than cattle, including humans.
    In case the muzzle point of the animal has something on it which can
cause the muzzle pattern or the lower lip of the animal to not be visible,
in that case, it would be useless to extract muzzle pattern and the                 3.7. Yolov4
identification process can be flawed, thus this class is necessary to avoid
any dirty muzzle pattern to be captured, and the system will be able to                 We have also trained on yolov4 for object detection, which shows
alert about the dirty muzzle of the animal.                                         considerably good mAP and no overfitting, following Fig. 7 shows the
                                                                                    training loss and mAP gain.
 • Not-cow:

    Some object detection models are likely to detect other animal faces




                          Fig. 2. Data Preparation.                                                          Fig. 3. Class Labels Analysis.

                                                                                5
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                    Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390




                                                         Fig. 4. A Sample from Annotated Data.


                                                                                cardinality of new features has been achieved by using collective
                                                                                convolution while the gradient-oriented routing does not change as
                                                                                shown in Fig. 8.
                                                                                    The process can increase learned features with the help of feature
                                                                                mapping, shuffling, and merging in a cardinality manner. Consequently,
                                                                                improve calculation and parameter usage. YOLOv7 is the most appro­
                                                                                priate object detector for the aggregate scaling approach. It can be
                                                                                applied to interlinked model architecture to compute modifications in
                                                                                the output width of the computational block. It is recommended to scale
                                                                                only depth in computational blocks. This approach can maintain the
                                                                                initial model design and structure properties. Re-parameterization
                                                                                techniques involve averaging a set of model weights to create a model
                                                                                that is more robust to the general patterns that it is trying to model. In
                                                                                research, there has been a recent focus on module-level re-
                                                                                parameterization where the piece of the network has its re-
                                                                                parameterization strategies. The YOLOv7 authors use gradient flow
                                                                                propagation paths to see which modules in the network should use re-
                                                                                parameterization strategies and which should not.
                                                                                    A state-of-the-art Yolov7 object detection model is the latest intro­
                                                                                duction to YOLO family. It has a few variants. Some of which we used for
                       Fig. 5. Detecto Loss Curve.
                                                                                training our data are:

    Even though yolov4 showed promising results with mAP of 100% yet             • Yolov7-tiny (training from scratch)
it was not considered final model for detection as its inference time was        • Yolov7x or Yolox (training using transfer learning)
high.                                                                            • Yolov7 (training from scratch)
                                                                                   a. Yolov7-tiny

3.8. Yolov7 - network Architecture
                                                                                    We performed training using transfer learning of pre-trained yolov7
                                                                                tiny model and it can be seen in the following Fig. 9, the overall mAP
    YOLOv7 presents the latest technology to support the multiple object
                                                                                achieved by yolov7-tiny on our dataset is 0.9946 or 99.46%. The loss
tracking (MOT) framework [31]. Yolov7 performs extremely well when
                                                                                curve is seen in the Fig. 10.
it comes to object detection. It detects objects more accurately and
                                                                                    There is no overfitting of the model as can be seen in the figures
swiftly than the previous versions. In this architecture enhanced

above and test results are present in Fig. 11.                                variant was able achieved 83% mean average precision after being
                                                                              trained from scratch.
b. Yolov7x                                                                        The YOLOv7 model is trained using a deep neural network that is
                                                                              specifically designed for object detection tasks. It comprises on three
   We also trained yolov7x model on our dataset and the training was          main segments: the backbone network, the neck network, and the
done from scratch, following figures: Fig. 12 & Fig. 13 show the ach­         detection head. The backbone network extracts high-level features from
ieved overall mAP and loss curve respectively. Yolov7x mAP is around          the input image, which are important in identifying objects. The neck
78% and the loss curve shows over fitting.                                    network refines the extracted features and integrates data from different
                                                                              scales and resolutions, which improves the model’s accuracy. Ulti­
c. Yolov7                                                                     mately, the detection head produces the final predictions, generating
                                                                              bounding box predictions and class probabilities for identified objects
    The Fig. 14 show how yolov7 model trained on our data and this yolo       using the improved features. These components are optimized using


                                                                          7
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                  Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390




                                                          Fig. 8. Yolov7 Model Architecture.


cutting-edge methods and convolutional layers to ensure optimal per­           all these, other yolov7 models can also be fine-tuned to achieve better
formance in object detection.                                                  results. The final model which is yolov7-tiny, is deployed on the cloud.
    Overfitting is visible in Fig. 15, loss curve of yolov7 shows slight           We trained several object detection models from YOLO object
overfitting of the model.                                                      detection family and Detecto, Yolo family include Yolov4, Yolov7,
    Once the training was completed Fig. 16, comparing results of all          Yolov7x, and Yolov7-tiny. The training was done on NVidia GTX 1050 Ti
trained models Table 3 showed that yolov7-tiny model was best among            4 GB GPU and Google Colab [32]. The training results were then

                                                                           8
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                    Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390




                                                       Fig. 9. YOLOv7-tiny mean Average Precision.


                                                                                described as follows and these confusion matrices defines and summa­
                                                                                rizes the detection model performance [33]. These confusion matrices
                                                                                show performance of the model on training set, validation set, and test
                                                                                set.

                                                                                 • Face Class

                                                                                    Face class represents dataset of 4945 images for training, 269 for
                                                                                validation, and 56 test instances. Our model correctly classifies all im­
                                                                                ages as shown in Table 4. Predicted results are same as actual.

                                                                                 • Nose Class

                                                                                    Nose class distributed as 3342 images for training, 181images for
                                                                                validation, and 39 for testing. Table 5 shows 99% training accuracy
                                                                                because of 34 images have been wrongly classified. While validation and
                                                                                testing accuracy is also 99%.

                                                                                 • Nose-Dirty Class
                    Fig. 10. YOLOv7-tiny Loss Curve.
                                                                                    Nose-Dirty class has been inserted to enhance model performance.
compared and we found that yolov7 has better overall performance even           2878 images allocated for training while 178 images for validation, and
though yolov4 had a mAP of 100% yet its inference time was highest              38 images were allocated for testing. Table 6 shows 99% accurate
among all of the models.                                                        training because of 33 images have been wrongly classified. Validation
    Table. 3 shows the comparison of different detection models that we         and testing accuracy is 99% and 100% respectively.
used, without transformation to ONNX.
    The yolov7 model is then transformed to ONNX (Open Neural                    • Not-Cow Class
Network Exchange) and is simplified which further improved inference
time and the ONNX model has inference time of 0.04 s and that too on                Not-Cow class added to prevent object selection other than Cow. It
CPU (Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 5122 CPU @ 3.60 GHz 3.59 GHz).                       consists of 3298 images for training while 158 images for validation.
    Based on comparison results and multiple test runs, we decided to           Table 7 shows 100% accuracy for training and validation, and 99% for
deploy yolov7-tiny object detection model on cloud as our default model         testing.
for detecting earlier defined classes, then use them to extract muzzle
pattern features, identify the animal, and finally decide whether the           e. Cattle Identification Process
claim for livestock insurance was fraudulent or not based on identifi­
cation results.                                                                    In our proposed model, the cattle Identification process comprises
                                                                                two stages. First to register the animal Table 8 & Fig. 17 with specified
d. Confusion matrices                                                           details such as identity tag and muzzle point feature images and sec­
                                                                                ondly how the system will process pattern matching for subsequent
    The individual class confusion matrices of yolov7-tiny model are            recognition of the same Animal. These processes are elaborated as

                                                                                              Identification string to be created for each animal. The Registration Tag
Model Comparison.
                                                                                              provides a referential key to be mapped with the visual for subsequent
  Models                  mAP                  Inference time        FPS                      retrieval of identity information.
  Yolov7 Tiny             0.9946               0.06 s                35 (On Average)
  Yolov7x                 0.7833               0.18 s                29 (On Average)          b. Video stream or Images
  Yolov7                  0.8347               0.21 s                19 (On Average)
  Yolov4                  1.0                  6.00 s                4 (On Average)
  Detecto                 0.94                 4.24 s                6 (On Average)
                                                                                                 Animal image is an essential parameter to be entered into the data­
                                                                                              base. The system will extract biometric features from the images. it can
                                                                                              be either a frame from a video or a single image.
follows:
                                                                                              c. Tag Validation
3.8.1. Animal registration
                                                                                                  Before starting up the animal registration process, the system vali­
a. Insertion for Animal Registration Tag                                                      dates the tag. Duplicate, unspecified, and the substandard tag will be
                                                                                              rejected.

                                                                                     swiftly and accurately.
Table 8
Algorithm for Animal Registration.
                                                                                      f. Detect nose within face area and mark as a nose.
  Algorithm 1 (Animal Registration):

  Input:                                                                                Next to marking the face area, system detect Nose on the animal’s
    TAG = Animal TAG;                                                                face. The same procedure is applied as used for face detection.
    stream = Video Stream of Animal / image of animal
  Output:
    image = Extracted muzzle point of Animal                                         g. Extract muzzle pattern from detect nose
    stored in database with TAG
    while stream do:validate                                                            At this stage, the system successfully detected the Nose area. Now
    (TAG)                                                                            extract the muzzle pattern as the required biometric identifier.
    frame ← stream.frame
    frame ← frame.reshape
    face ← Detect(frame, “Face”)                                                     h. Update database with muzzle pattern and Tag ID.
    nose ← Detect(face, “Nose”)
    nose ← ExtractFeature(nose)                                                         Finally, extracted muzzle pattern mapped Tag ID of the same cattle
    image = WriteDB(nose, TAG)display
                                                                                     and store the information in the designated database management sys­
    (image)
    end                                                                              tem. Show cattle image after a successful registration.

                                                                                     3.8.2. Recognizing the animal
d. Image reshapes as per defined standard parameters                                    Once the nose gets detected, the scale invariant feature transform
                                                                                     algorithm (SIFT) is used to get key points and descriptors of muzzle point
   Image resizing process perform after tag validation, Animal image to              image, i.e., query image, and it also finds key points and descriptors of
be optimized to make to compatible for feature extraction procedures.                the images of nose/muzzle points of different animals stored, Table 9 &
                                                                                     Fig. 18.
e. Detect face area and mark as a face.
                                                                                     3.8.3. Scale-Invariant feature Transform (SIFT)
   Animal face recognition is essential to detect face and then proceed                 It is commonly used to extract distinguishing features from photos,
to identify muzzle point. We use Yolov7 to perform face detection                    which enables accurate object detection and image matching. In order to




                                                          Fig. 17. Animal Registration Algorithm.

                                                                                12
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                              Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


Table 9                                                                           DoG pictures.
Algorithm for Animal Recognition.                                                     The programme employs additional criteria to precisely localise the
  Algorithm 2 (Animal Recognition):                                               critical points. This includes eliminating extrema or low contrast key
                                                                                  points, as well as key-point localization triggered by rejecting files and
  Input:
    stream = Video Stream of Animal / image of animal                             edges. According to the equation, files and edges in the context of SIFT
  Output:                                                                         correspond to particular properties that must be disregarded during key-
    image, TAG = Recognized Animal with TAG                                       point localization:
    while stream do:
    frame ← stream.frame                                                          |D(̂x )〈0.03                                                                        (2)
    frame ← frame.reshape
    face ← Detect(frame, “Face”)                                                      Furthermore, edges and unstable key points are rejected by
    nose ← Detect(face, “Nose”)                                                   analyzing the eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix H. Letting α be the
    nose ← ExtractFeature(nose)                                                   larger eigenvalue and β be the smaller eigenvalue, the trace and deter­
    image, TAG = Recognize(nose)display
                                                                                  minant of H are calculated as:
    (image, TAG)
    end                                                                           Tr(H) = Dxx + Dyy = α + β                                                           (3)
                                                                                                       (         )2
find key points—areas of the image that are unaffected by adjustments             Det(H) = Dxx Dyy −       Dxy        = αβ                                            (4)
to scale, rotation, and illumination—SIFT examines image features.                   Let r = α/β.
                                                                                     So, α = rβ
a) Focus Localization                                                               ( )
                                                                                  Tr H 2     (α + β)2   (rβ + β)2   (r + 1)2
                                                                                          =           =           =                                                   (5)
   To precisely locate the key points in the image, the SIFT algorithm’s          Det(H)        αβ         rβ 2
                                                                                                                        r
key-point localization is used. It is performed by examining the image’s
                                                                                      The algorithm checks to see if the ratio r is smaller than 10 before
scale-space representation, which is created by applying a number of
                                                                                  computing it as r=/. Key points are disregarded if this requirement is not
scale-space transformations. The image is scaled to several octaves, each
                                                                                  met when the eigenvalues are almost equal, which denotes a flat region.
of which is half the size of the preceding octave, in order to locate the
                                                                                      The next step in the SIFT method is orientation assignment after each
potential feature sites. Within each octave, a scale-space pyramid is
                                                                                  key point’s scale, location, and orientation have been established. The
constructed by convolving the images with Gaussian blur filters. The
                                                                                  algorithm now makes sure that the extracted characteristics are
blurred images are represented as:
                                                                                  rotation-invariant as well as scale-invariant. To achieve this invariance,
L(x, y, σ) = G(x, y, σ)*l(x, y)                                       (1)         an orientation is allocated to each key point.
                                                                                      Each key point is given an orientation by SIFT after it has examined
    Where x,y are the variable coordinates and σ is the “scale” param­
                                                                                  the local image region around it. The algorithm computes gradient
eter, G(x,y,σ) is the Gaussian function, and I(x,y) is the original image.
                                                                                  magnitudes and orientations of the image pixels within this region. The
    Next, the adjacent blurred images inside each octave are subtracted
                                                                                  dominant orientation in the region is then identified, and the key point is
to create the Difference of Gaussians (DoG) images. The local scale-space
                                                                                  assigned that orientation. This step ensures that the subsequent com­
extrema, which correlate to possible key locations, are shown by the
                                                                                  putations are rotationally invariant.




                                                         Fig. 18. Animal Recognition Algorithm.

                                                                             13
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                       Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


b) Computational Cost                                                                  Procedures are applied to detect the face and nose area to get the
                                                                                   latest muzzle pattern. The system will look up the muzzle pattern with
    The scale-space pyramid construction, DoG computation, and                     the stored information in the database. In case the pattern is matched,
eigenvalue analysis used in the SIFT algorithm’s key-point localization            the system will get the concerned Tag ID.
step add significant processing cost. However, this cost is required to
guarantee the reliability and exactness of the essential points that are           3.8.4. Parameter settings
discovered.                                                                            A comprehensive account of the parameter settings employed
    It is pertinent to remember that the computational cost of key-point           throughout our methodology.
localization is influenced by the size and complexity of the input image               For YOLOv7, the following parameters were utilized:
as well as the desired degree of scale-space representation. More octaves              Input image size: 640x640.
and scales are needed for huge photos, which increases computing cost.                 Learning rate: 0.0001.
SIFT is now computationally practical for a variety of real-world ap­                  Batch size: 16.
plications thanks to developments in technology and optimization                       Number of epochs: 300.
approaches.                                                                            Optimizer: Adam.
                                                                                       Loss function: Cross-entropy.
c) Orientation Assignment and Key-point Descriptor                                     Regarding the SIFT algorithm, the following parameters were
                                                                                   utilized:
    The SIFT algorithm assigns an orientation to each key point after key-             Number of octaves: 4.
point localization, making each key point rotation invariant. The algo­                Step size: 1.
rithm’s ability to match important spots in various orientations is                    Contrast threshold: 0.03 (Kept to default).
improved by this orientation assignment.                                               Edge threshold: 10.
    The SIFT algorithm then computes a descriptor for each key point                   RANSAC threshold: 3.
after determining the scale, location, and orientation of each key point.              For FLANN (Fast Library for Approximate Nearest Neighbors), the
The key point’s immediate surroundings are captured by the descriptor,             following parameters were used:
which results in a highly distinctive depiction that is resistant to varia­            K: 7.
tions in scale, rotation, and illumination.                                            Tree type: K-D tree.
    A region surrounding each key point is specified, often in the form of             Number of trees: 6.
a square or circular neighborhood, in order to calculate the key-point                 Distance metric: Euclidean distance.
descriptor. A histogram of the gradient orientations is produced                       We recognize the importance of reporting all parameter configura­
within each of the smaller sub regions or bins that make up this region.           tions to ensure transparency and reproducibility of our methodology. By
The gradient orientations reveal details about the boundaries and                  providing these details, we aim to enhance the comprehensiveness and
texture of the local image structure.                                              accuracy of our paper.
    Each pixel within the sub regions has its gradient magnitudes and
orientations calculated, and these values are weighted by a Gaussian               4. Results ad discussion
function centered at the critical point. This weighting prioritizes the
gradients nearer the focal point, obtaining the most pertinent local data.             The best options and combinations of the strategies have been
    The weighted gradient magnitudes are then added together into                  carefully evaluated for the proposed plan. The requirement for an
orientation bins to create the histogram of gradient orientations. The             automated and non-intrusive method to capture the muzzle point of the
key-point descriptor is formed from this histogram and is often displayed          cattle served as the driving force for the choice of YOLOv7 as the object
as a high-dimensional feature vector. The number of bins utilized in the           identification framework. Other conventional techniques, including
orientation histogram and other factors set during the descriptor                  personally photographing animals up close or employing restraints, can
computation determine how dimensional the descriptor is.                           be time-consuming, labor-intensive, and possibly upsetting to the ani­
    The resulting key-point descriptors are strong and distinctive,                mals. We were able to record the animals from a safe distance while
allowing for quick matching and scene identification. To determine                 minimizing disruption to their natural behavior by utilizing YOLOv7 to
which critical points in distinct photos match up best, they can be                automate the procedure of finding the muzzle point.
compared using a variety of distance measures, such as Euclidean dis­                  Additionally, the difficulties with other well-known techniques like
tance or cosine similarity.                                                        CNN, RCNN, RESNET, and VGG16 that did not deliver adequate out­
                                                                                   comes in terms of muzzle pattern identification led to the choice to use
d) FLANN Based Matcher                                                             Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) for muzzle pattern feature
                                                                                   extraction. For extracting distinguishing features from photos, particu­
   FLANN-based matcher or Brute Force matcher is then used to match                larly when there are variations in scale, rotation, and viewpoint, SIFT is
the key points and descriptors of the query image with the images in the           a well-known and reliable technique. We deployed SIFT to the muzzle
data, and if it matches with the image of the animal in the data, it will          region in an effort to record distinctive patterns that could be used for
return the tag associated with that matched image in data, thus,                   precise animal identification and recognition.
recognizing/identifying the animal.                                                    Our system can detect face and muzzle point of cow/buffalo with
                                                                                   mAP of 99%, not only that but this system has the capability to differ­
 i. Video stream or Images                                                         entiate cows/buffalos from other cattle as well as humans. The inference
                                                                                   time is also remarkable as we have used the state of the art yolov7 object
    On the spot image/video capture to identify registered cattle.                 detection model. As far as recognition is concerned, the feature match­
                                                                                   ing algorithm FLANN is used for muzzle point pattern recognition. The
ii. Reshape as per defined standard parameters                                     system was able to recognize the animal with 100% accuracy. The
                                                                                   testing was done by registering the animal in the system using an image
   Same procedures have been applied to the captured image as already              and then tested with different images in different environments. Images
performed during the registration process.                                         of a total of 500 animals were used to evaluate recognition algorithm
                                                                                   and it recognized all the animals with 100% accuracy beating the
   iii. Match image with stored muzzle pattern and Tag ID.                         humans because at one point we were not able to recognize the animal

                                                                              14
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                         Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


by just looking at the picture but the system successfully recognized the
animal.

4.1. Proposed model

    The Fig. 19 represents our proposed model for the purpose of tack­
ling livestock insurance fraud by biometrically identifying cattle
through their muzzle point patterns.

4.2. Confusion Matrix

    In this research study, though our prime objective is to present a most
efficient and effective model for cattle.
    Identification it would be helpful for biometric identification of other
Animals having muzzle pattern. Fig. 20 is the confusion matrix showing
the performance of the system in identification of the animal.
    Following is the Table 10, enlisting animals that have unique
distinguishable features:                                                                         Fig. 20. Cattle Identification Confusion Matrix.
    Our proposed model takes cattle images/videos in real-time and yolo
object detection model extracts muzzle point in two steps, first is to              registration. In case of recognition, the extracted features are then
extract the head/face of the animal in order to make sure that only cow,            transferred to the matcher algorithm which matches the muzzle point
buffalo, bull, and/or a calf is present in the scene, next once the muzzle          features with already stored muzzle features in database and in case of a
point is taken out, its features are extracted and stores in case of animal




                                                                  Fig. 19. Proposed Model.

                                                                               15
M. Ahmad et al.                                                                                                                Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100390


Table 10                                                                                    5. Conclusion
Animal Unique Features.
  Sr. No                         Animal                          Unique Feature                 Accurate identification of cattle is very critical and remains chal­
                                                                                            lenging for veterinarian and livestock Insurer. They have been facing
  1                              Cows                            Muzzle/Nose Pattern
  2                              Bulls                           Muzzle/Nose Pattern        problems for registration and subsequent accurate recognition of regis­
  3                              Calves                          Muzzle/Nose Pattern        tered animals. Livestock insurance frauds not only entail financial losses
  4                              Buffalos                        Muzzle/Nose Pattern        to the insurers but they also limit the capacity of livestock Insurers to
  5                              Horses                          Muzzle/Nose Pattern        provide services to the potential clients.
  6                              Goats                           Muzzle/Nose Pattern
  7                              Sheep                           Muzzle/Nose Patter
                                                                                                In this paper, we introduced a unique approach based on Yolov7
  8                              Cats                            Nose Pattern               techniques of object detection. It detects objects more accurately and
  9                              Dogs                            Nose Pattern               swiftly than the previous versions. Our dataset includes 9400 images.
  10                             Lions                           Whisker Holes              Total annotated dataset comprises 15,416 class labels representing all
                                                                                            the four classes face, nose, dirty nose and not cow. Then had to be split
                                                                                            into training ~ 94%, validation ~ 5%, and testing 1%.
hit, it returns the tag of the animal which can assure genuineness of the
                                                                                                Images of a total of 500 animals were used to evaluate the recogni­
insurance claim, but if the matcher does not recognize, then that would
                                                                                            tion algorithm and it recognized all the animals with 100% accuracy as
mean that the animal was not registered in the system which subse­
                                                                                            we have kept the threshold of recognition by considering the sensitivity
quently tells that the claim for livestock insurance was fraudulent.
                                                                                            of the risk involved Animal is considered as recognized if the recognition
    The algorithms for cattle identification systems are compared in
                                                                                            algorithm matches at most 10 key points and descriptors. But this can be
Table 11, along with the detection and identification accuracy rates. The
                                                                                            changed which can change the accuracy of animal identification.
first system uses the Fuzzy-KNN algorithm in combination with the
                                                                                                The proposed model may be applied to resolve cattle identification
Hybrid Feature Extraction and Classification approach to achieve an
                                                                                            current issues, and also lead to exploring other identification technolo­
accuracy rate of 96.74% with a loss rate of 3.26%. However, as animals
                                                                                            gies to protect livestock from serious diseases and avoid financial loss.
tend to flee, collecting muzzle point photographs of cattle for this
                                                                                            Likewise, it may be helpful for Livestock Insurers to substantiate valid
technique can be difficult.
                                                                                            claims by accurate identification of the exactly insured animal. We are
    The second system achieves a 95.13% accuracy rate with a 4.87%
                                                                                            confident that this research work will provide a firm basis for future
loss rate using the BoHoG and LBP Histogram algorithms. However, they
                                                                                            studies on the identification of other animals having muzzle patterns as
use the watershed approach to estimate the location of the muzzle point
                                                                                            listed in Table 10. The application of more advanced methodologies
in the image’s center, which might not be precise in real-time scenarios.
                                                                                            with an extended system for a huge dataset is highly recommended as a
    With the help of the VGGNet-DCNN algorithm and the Cattle Face
                                                                                            research area for future work. Researchers should focus on techniques to
Recognition Method, the third system achieves an identification accu­
                                                                                            provide more accurate robust identification irrespective of the muzzle
racy rate of 70.00%. The YOLO algorithm, which is used by the fourth
                                                                                            pattern quality.
system, has a detection accuracy rate of 96.00%. However, because they
                                                                                                Funding statement
can be switched or faked, ear tags are not a reliable method for identi­
                                                                                                This research program is partially funded by the United Insurance
fying cows.
                                                                                            Company of Pakistan Ltd. in collaboration with United Software and
    The fifth system achieves a 93.30% identification accuracy rate with
                                                                                            Technologies International (Pvt) Ltd. under Grant No.UICL-06–2022/
a 6.70% loss rate by utilizing a Unified Deep Learning Architecture
                                                                                            USTI-RND-006/11/2022.
method with BiLSTM and Inception-V3 CNN algorithms. However, after
a certain frame length, the accuracy based on video frame length may
not improve or even decline.                                                                CRediT authorship contribution statement
    The sixth system uses YOLO and Few-Shot Deep Transfer Learning
algorithms for Automated Muzzle Detection and Biometric Identifica­                             Munir Ahmad: Resources, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft.
tion, achieving a 99.11% detection accuracy rate with a 0.99% loss rate.                    Sagheer Abbas: Resources, Supervision. Areej Fatima: Resources,
    Our proposed algorithm, which uses YOLOV7 and Muzzle Pattern                            Writing – original draft. Taher M. Ghazal: Formal analysis, Writing –
Feature Matching algorithms, achieved exceptional results. It achieved a                    original draft, Writing – review & editing. Meshal Alharbi: Writing –
detection accuracy rate of 99.50% with a 0.50% loss rate and a perfect                      original draft, Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Adnan Khan:
identification accuracy rate of 100.00% with 0.00% loss rate. The pro­                      Formal analysis, Supervision. Nouh Sabri Elmitwally: .
posed deep learning-based systems using YOLO algorithm demonstrated
superior performance in terms of detection and identification accuracy                      Declaration of Competing Interest
rates.

Table 11
Comparing Proposed Model with Different Research Approaches.
  Sr.      Title                                                  Algorithms                Detection        Detection Loss   Identification          Identification Loss
  No                                                                                        Accuracy Rate    Rate (%)         Accuracy Rate (%)       Rate (%)
                                                                                            (%)

  1        Hybrid Feature Extraction and Classification           Fuzzy-KNN                  –                –               96.74                    3.26
  2        Biometric for Cattle Identification using Muzzle       BoHoG and LBP              –                –               95.13                    4.87
           Patterns                                               Histogram
  3        Cattle Face Recognition Method Based on Parameter      VGGNet-DCNN                –                –               70.00                   30.00
           Transfer and Deep Learning
  4        Cow Identification System Using Ear Tag Recognition    YOLO                      96.00            4.00              –                       –
  5        Automated Individual Cattle Identification Using       BiLSTM.                    –               –                93.30                    6.70
           Video Data: A Unified Deep Learning Architecture       Inception-V3 CNN
           Approach
  6        Automated Muzzle Detection and Biometric               YOLO & Few-Shot Deep      99.11            0.99              –                       –
           Identification                                         Transfer Learning
